metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. `pytest src/rebar/attest/test_registry.py -k 'tampered or unknown_scheme' -v`) and name the test file so done-ness is unambiguously verifiable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is given for any acceptance criterion. All three ACs reference 'test' in prose but provide no pytest invocation, no test-file path, and no assertion anchor — leaving done-ness unverifiable without further interpretation.
findings.location: AC2: 'a test asserts a record whose in-band scheme/keyid is tampered still verifies under the policy-pinned scheme'; AC3: 'Unknown/mismatched scheme for a kind fails closed (test)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Verification' section: `pytest src/rebar/attest/test_registry.py -v` should show: `test_registry_resolves_kind` (AC1), `test_tampered_inband_scheme_still_verifies` (AC2), `test_unknown_kind_fails_closed` (AC3). All three must be GREEN.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies namespace is enforced during dispatch (e.g., an envelope claiming the correct kind but wrong namespace is rejected), or remove namespace from the registry schema description if it is out of scope for this plan.
findings.finding: The Context and AC1 both include `namespace` as a field in the registry entry ({scheme, namespace, trust-root/trust_root}), but no acceptance criterion verifies that namespace is actually consulted, enforced, or checked during the verify() dispatch — leaving a described deliverable with no covering test criterion.
findings.location: Context paragraph and AC1 vs AC2, AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'envelope' and 'trust_root' concretely in terms of the existing data model: show how a stored `{manifest, signature, key_id, algorithm}` record maps to 'envelope', and clarify whether 'trust_root' replaces or wraps the existing environment-HMAC key (and how existing call sites adopt the new API).
findings.finding: The `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` API uses undefined abstractions ('envelope', 'trust_root') that have no corresponding data structures in the codebase. Existing signature records are plain dicts with `manifest`/`signature`/`key_id`; the trust root is the environment HMAC key resolved from `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY`/`.signing-key`, not a caller-supplied argument. The plan gives no bridging specification from the existing data model to the new API, so the proposed mechanism cannot integrate with any existing call site.
findings.location: AC: `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` dispatches on the policy scheme; Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the reconciliation rule for `trust_root`: state whether `verify()`'s `trust_root` parameter overrides the registry, is validated against it, or whether the parameter should be removed and always sourced from the registry.
findings.finding: The Context states `trust_root` is 'pinned out-of-band' in the registry (implying the registry is authoritative), but AC2 defines `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` accepting `trust_root` as a caller-supplied parameter — no reconciliation rule is specified for what happens when the caller's `trust_root` differs from the registry entry.
findings.location: Context ('pinned out-of-band'); Acceptance Criteria AC2 ('verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)')
findings.suggested_fix: If the registry is the single source of truth, remove `trust_root` from the `verify()` signature and always use the registry-pinned value. If caller override is intentional, add an explicit rule: 'caller-supplied trust_root is validated against the registry entry; a mismatch fails closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the trust_root sourcing contradiction: either remove trust_root from verify()'s signature (let the registry supply it exclusively) or explicitly state that the parameter is validated against the registry-pinned value, and update both Context and AC2 to describe the same model.
findings.finding: The Context states trust-root is 'pinned out-of-band' (implying the registry is the authoritative source), but AC2 defines verify() as accepting trust_root as a caller-supplied parameter — an implementer could read these as two different trust-root sourcing models, one registry-driven and one caller-driven.
findings.location: Context paragraph vs AC2 verify() signature
findings.suggested_fix: If the registry is the single source of truth (as Context implies), change AC2's signature to verify(kind, envelope) and have the implementation look up trust_root from the registry internally. If caller-supplied override is intentional, add a note in Context clarifying that the caller-supplied value is cross-checked against the registry, not used blindly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define what `namespace` means in the attestation verification context with a concrete consumer AC, or remove it from the per-kind policy shape until a real requirement exists.
findings.finding: The `namespace` field in `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` is undefined in the plan and has no existing consumer or production call-site in the codebase; it appears to be a hypothetical forward-looking placeholder with no current done-definition.
findings.location: AC line 1: `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the semantics, valid value vocabulary, and runtime usage of the `namespace` field in the policy struct (or remove it from the schema if unused in v1).
findings.finding: The `namespace` field in the per-kind policy struct `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` is named but never given any semantics, value vocabulary, or usage rule anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Context ('a registry mapping each attestation `kind` to `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}`'); Acceptance Criteria AC1

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete value vocabulary for the `scheme` field in the policy struct (e.g., an Enum or a set of string constants) co-located with the `registry.py` schema definition.
findings.finding: The `scheme` field in the per-kind policy struct is referenced as the dispatch key throughout the plan ('dispatches on the policy scheme', 'unknown/mismatched scheme fails closed') but its value vocabulary — i.e., what values `scheme` can take — is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2 and AC3; Context ('dispatches on the policy-pinned scheme')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The `scheme` field is one of a fixed set of string constants defined as a `SchemeId` enum (or module-level constants) in `registry.py`, e.g., `HMAC_SHA256 = "hmac-sha256"`. The initial table contains exactly one entry mapping each existing attestation kind to `HMAC_SHA256`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to specify that a `kind` not present in the registry table also fails closed — distinct from 'mismatched scheme' — and add a test for it.
findings.finding: The plan specifies fail-closed behavior for an 'unknown/mismatched scheme for a kind' (AC3) but does not specify the fallback when the `kind` itself is not present in the registry — a distinct, more fundamental failure mode.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to: 'Unknown kind (not in registry) OR mismatched in-band scheme fails closed (two tests, one per failure mode).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the policy table is hardcoded or loaded from a configurable source (e.g., TOML/env), and if it will evolve, make it configurable rather than hardcoded.
findings.finding: The policy table is described as 'static' and 'pinned out-of-band,' but the plan does not specify whether it is hardcoded in source or loaded from a configurable source (file, env, config). Attestation kinds, namespaces, and trust roots are expected to evolve as new producers onboard, making this a threshold that will change.
findings.location: AC1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` resolves `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}` from a static policy table
findings.suggested_fix: Load the policy table from a `policy.toml` or equivalent config file (with a well-defined schema), falling back to a hardcoded default only for tests. Document the loading mechanism in the module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the static policy table is initialized (e.g., module-level dict literal vs. loaded from config), and describe a test-safe injection mechanism (e.g., a `build_registry(policy_dict)` factory or a pytest fixture that patches the table) so tests can configure policies without mutating global state.
findings.finding: The 'static policy table' is module-level shared state, but the plan never specifies how it is created or initialized: hardcoded dict literal, loaded from a config file at import time, or injected at startup. This leaves the 'who creates the state and when' lifecycle sub-criterion unmet and creates a practical gap for test isolation — tests that need to exercise different policy configurations have no described injection/override mechanism.
findings.location: AC line 1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` resolves `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}` from a static policy table
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `Registry` class (or `build_registry(mapping: dict) -> Registry`) that takes the policy dict as a constructor argument, making the singleton explicit and swappable in tests. The default global instance can be `REGISTRY = build_registry(DEFAULT_POLICY)` but tests construct their own instance without touching the global.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and test) asserting that `verify()` fails closed when the policy-pinned scheme is correct but the cryptographic signature is invalid (corrupt bytes or wrong key), so the suite covers the full failure-path of the verification chain, not only scheme-dispatch failures.
findings.finding: The testing plan has no acceptance criterion for cryptographic verification failure (e.g., a valid kind and policy-pinned scheme but a corrupt or wrong-key signature): only scheme-selection failure (AC3) is covered, leaving the core signature-validation failure path untested for a security primitive.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('Unknown/mismatched scheme for a kind fails closed')
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` raises/returns a verification failure when the envelope's signature is cryptographically invalid under the policy-pinned scheme (e.g., signature bytes are corrupted or the key does not match trust_root); a test exercises this path explicitly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` registry with ≥3 concrete call-sites requiring different schemes or trust-roots, OR reduce to a simple constant/guard (matching ADR 0030 / grounding/semantic.py precedent) until a second real scheme exists.
findings.finding: The proposed `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` per-kind policy table is a premature abstraction: the entire codebase has exactly one signing scheme (HMAC-SHA256, hard-coded as a module constant in signing.py) and one trust-root model (the environment signing key), yielding zero existing call-sites that require multi-scheme or multi-trust-root dispatch. Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 before a registry abstraction is warranted; none exist.
findings.location: AC line 1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` resolves `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the per-kind registry with a single-scheme guard at the verify entry point (e.g. assert/raise on any envelope 'algorithm' that isn't HMAC-SHA256) — same anti-alg-confusion effect, zero premature abstraction. Introduce the registry only when a second production scheme lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `trust_root` in `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` is distinct from the existing `key` parameter in `verify_record()`, and if not, reuse the existing seam rather than introducing a parallel trust-root injection path.
findings.finding: The `trust_root` parameter in `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` partially rebuilds the existing `signing_key()` / environment-key model: the codebase already has one trust-root concept (the environment HMAC key, resolved by `signing_key()` and threaded through `verify_record()`). Introducing `trust_root` as a per-call parameter creates a parallel trust-root injection path with no current multi-trust-root requirement.
findings.location: AC line 2: `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` dispatches on the policy scheme

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the RFC 8725 alg-confusion threat actually applies to the current envelope format (specifically: is there an in-band algorithm field that the verifier currently selects on?), and if not, narrow the AC to the concrete invariant that does apply (e.g. 'the verifier must never read the signing scheme from the envelope record').
findings.finding: The anti-alg-confusion / RFC 8725 framing is cargo-cult applied to the current HMAC system: RFC 8725 alg-confusion is a JWT-specific concern where the algorithm is negotiated in-band in the JOSE header. The current signing system hard-codes ALGORITHM='HMAC-SHA256' as a module constant and `verify_record()` never reads or dispatches on the envelope's 'algorithm' field — there is no in-band algorithm selection to confuse. The plan imports a JWT-world defense into a non-JWT architecture where the attack vector doesn't exist.
findings.location: Context section: 'so the verifier never selects the scheme from attacker-controlled record data (anti alg-confusion, RFC 8725)'
findings.suggested_fix: If the real goal is to document and test the invariant that verify never reads the algorithm from the envelope, add a unit test asserting that tampering `record['algorithm']` does not change the verify outcome — no new registry needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `9fd5-0074-a995-43e6` (DSSE envelope ticket) to this ticket's `deps` so the `src/rebar/attest/` package is guaranteed to exist before `registry.py` is implemented.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` lives inside the `src/rebar/attest/` package, which is created by sibling ticket 9fd5-0074-a995-43e6 (status: `in_progress`). That ticket is NOT listed in this ticket's `deps[]` (which is empty), so there is no guarantee the package init exists when this ticket is implemented. If 518b lands before 9fd5, `registry.py` would be placed in a non-existent package directory.
findings.location: Ticket 518b-0558-fd18-4713 `deps: []` / AC1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) state a positive rationale for why a new `rebar.attest.registry` module and multi-scheme dispatch are needed beyond what `signing.verify_record` already provides (e.g. future asymmetric scheme support, cross-kind namespace isolation), or (b) reframe the plan to add an explicit algorithm-assertion + test directly in the existing `signing.py`/`verify_record` path.
findings.finding: The stated problem ('verifier never selects the scheme from attacker-controlled record data, anti alg-confusion RFC 8725') is already solved in the existing codebase: `signing.verify_record` always calls `compute_signature` with the hardcoded `ALGORITHM = 'HMAC-SHA256'` constant — the stored `record.get('algorithm')` field is echo/audit metadata only and is never used to select a verification path. A clearly-better alternative is to (a) make this existing defense explicit with a documented assertion and a direct test against `signing.verify_record`, rather than building a parallel, disconnected registry module in a new package.
findings.location: Context section: 'the verifier never selects the scheme from attacker-controlled record data'; AC line 2 tamper test
findings.suggested_fix: If the goal is strictly anti-alg-confusion for the current HMAC system: add `assert record.get('algorithm') in (None, ALGORITHM)` (or a reject-not-certified branch) in `verify_record` before the HMAC computation, and add a test asserting a record with a tampered `algorithm` field does not certify. This is 3 lines + 1 test, fully aligned with the existing module, zero new package.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` registry against the team's Rule-of-Three policy (ADR 0030): either cite ≥2 concrete distinct schemes that will coexist in this plan's scope, or reduce `registry.py` to a simple kind→policy lookup without scheme-dispatch abstraction until a second scheme is real.
findings.finding: The `{scheme, namespace, trust_root}` registry is a novel architectural pattern the team's own Rule-of-Three policy (documented in `src/rebar/grounding/semantic.py` and ADR 0030) says not to introduce until ≥3 real call-sites justify it. The codebase currently has exactly one signing scheme (HMAC-SHA256, `signing.py` line 54) and two attestation kinds (`plan-review`, `completion-verifier`) both using that scheme identically. No second scheme or third call-site exists or is cited in the plan. The plan commits to the registry architecture without justifying why it is warranted now rather than at the point a second scheme actually materialises.
findings.location: AC line 1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` resolves `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}`; Context section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the scheme-dispatch registry with a simpler kind→`{namespace, trust_root}` policy map (scheme remains the single constant `HMAC-SHA256` from `signing.py`). Add the scheme-dispatch layer only in the ticket/plan that actually introduces a second signing scheme.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the allowed values for `scheme` (e.g. an enum or string literals like `'jwt' | 'ssh'`) and explicitly declare whether the policy table is a hardcoded `dict` in `registry.py`, an imported config module, or a file, so the executor needs no design decision.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what values 'scheme' can take (e.g. 'jwt', 'ssh', 'x509', a fully-qualified class name) nor the storage format of the static policy table (hardcoded dict, TOML file, env var). An executor must make a design choice about both before writing a single line of registry.py, which is a deferred decision disguised as a stated requirement.
findings.location: AC1: `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` resolves `kind -> {scheme, namespace, trust_root}` from a static policy table; Context: 'pinned out-of-band'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line policy-table example to the plan, e.g.: `REGISTRY = { 'attestation-v1': {'scheme': 'jwt-es256', 'namespace': 'rebar.attest', 'trust_root': TRUST_ROOT_ENV} }` and state that `scheme` is a string matching one of the verifier adapter names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the anti-algorithm-confusion rationale (RFC 8725), the decision to pin scheme/namespace/trust_root out-of-band, and the threat model it addresses.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel security-architectural decision — domain-separation via a static registry to prevent algorithm-confusion attacks (RFC 8725) — but includes no mention of capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc. This is a non-obvious pattern whose rationale will not be recoverable from the code alone.
findings.location: Context section: 'domain-separation core … so the verifier never selects the scheme from attacker-controlled record data (anti alg-confusion, RFC 8725)'
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0001-attest-registry-alg-confusion.md` capturing: context (RFC 8725 alg-confusion), decision (static registry, not envelope-driven), consequences (new kinds require registry entry), and alternatives considered (envelope-driven scheme selection and why it was rejected).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the Context or Approach section explaining why a new `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` module (rather than extending `src/rebar/signing.py`) is the right fit — e.g., domain-separation boundary, future asymmetric support, or a specific gap in the current module.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why a new top-level package `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` is the right location and abstraction boundary, rather than extending the existing `src/rebar/signing.py` or `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`. The absence of rationale is a G6 criterion requirement.
findings.location: Context section and overall plan structure; new module path `src/rebar/attest/registry.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion: either name the specific second scheme `verify()` dispatches to (with its implementation source) and add it to scope, or acknowledge that single-scheme dispatch is a no-op and defer the dispatch layer.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `verify()` will 'dispatch on the policy scheme' but never identifies what concrete scheme values other than HMAC-SHA256 are available to dispatch to. The codebase has no second scheme implementation, and no library providing one is cited or verified. This is an unverified capability assertion on a security-critical code path.
findings.location: AC line 2: `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root)` dispatches on the policy scheme

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
